exemplar.title: The Relationship between The mass and terminal velocity of an object in water

detailedExemplarData.title: The Relationship between The mass and terminal velocity of an object in water

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Raw and processed data are clearly organised in tables with units and some stated uncertainties.
A graph is supplied with axes, units and a power-law fit, making the analysis communicable.
weaknesses: Uncertainties are inconsistently applied (Â±0.1 s vs. Â±0.05 s) and not propagated through every calculation.
Significant omissions in uncertainty analysis (mass uncertainties, fit parameters) and inconsistent significant-figure usage reduce precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although data recording and processing are communicated clearly (band 3â4), the quality of processing shows significant omissions and inaccuracies. A score of 2 reflects the lower half of the 1â2 band when compressed to the 10-point total, recognising clear presentation but penalising incomplete uncertainty treatment and precision errors.

strengths: The research question is placed within a highly specific and appropriate context, with clear independent and dependent variables.
Purposeful background theory (drag equation, buoyancy, Stokesâ law limitations) directly frames the investigation.
Apparatus list and numbered procedure are sufficiently detailed to allow reproduction with few ambiguities.
weaknesses: Minor procedural details are missing (exact water temperature, camera frame rate, marking method), which could affect reproducibility.
Methodological considerations are described but not fully explained (e.g., flow-regime assumptions for the drag equation).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Based on strand assessments, the research design meets the 3â4 band overall (clear context and reproducible method with few ambiguities, but limited explanation of methodological considerations). A mid-band score of 3 reflects strong contextual framing and clear procedures, tempered by the remaining ambiguities and lack of deeper methodological explanation.

strengths: The conclusion logically describes that heavier masses yield higher velocities and links to the derived theoretical relationship.
There is awareness of low correlation (0.79) and experimental limitations in the discussion.
weaknesses: Conclusion is stated with minimal quantitative supportâno comparison of predicted vs. actual ratios or confidence intervals.
Comparison to the accepted scientific context is superficial and lacks depth or justification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is described and linked to analysis but is not fully consistent or quantitatively supported (band 1â2 when scaled). A minimal score of 1 reflects the limited justification and context comparison, acknowledging the correct logical link but noting the superficial treatment.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses are identified with explained relative impacts (e.g., collisions, fall distance, retrieval issues).
Improvements are realistic, relevant and accompanied by explanations of how they address each weakness (e.g., PVC pipe, retrieval string).
weaknesses: Presentation of limitations and improvements could be better structured (separate sections or tables).
No discussion of resource or environmental considerations when recommending new apparatus.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands are firmly in the 5â6 band, with explained weaknesses and explained improvements. A high score of 4 reflects top-band performance in evaluation, recognizing comprehensive and justified critique while adjusting for the overall 10-point cap.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is clearly phrased, identifies both independent and dependent variables, and is rooted in a very specific scenario (a plastic egg of variable mass descending through water).  The introduction supplies purposeful background theory (drag equation, buoyancy, Stokesâ Law limitations) that directly frames the investigation, so the reader knows exactly why this particular system was chosen.  Because that context is narrowly linked to the apparatus actually used, the descriptor for the 5-6 band is met.

Uncertainties in time and distance are quoted and one example of propagation is shown; error bars appear on the graph.  However, propagation is not carried through for every data point and uncertainties in mass, velocity fit, or gradient are omitted.  These âsignificant omissionsâ place the work in the 3-4 band, lower half.

Controlled-variable table, uncontrolled-variable table and justification of each show that the student has thought about relevant factors; however, the discussion stops at description.  There is no deeper explanation of how each factor would quantitatively influence the results, so the strand fulfils âdescribedâ rather than âexplained,â placing it in the 3-4 band, nearer the lower end.

Three concrete weaknesses are identified (collisions, insufficient fall distance, awkward retrieval) and for each the student explicitly states the effect on velocity measurement (e.g., collisions remove kinetic energy, short cylinder prevents reaching terminal velocity).  The relative impact on accuracy is therefore explained, satisfying the top band.

The student lists apparatus, gives a numbered procedure, and specifies numerical dimensions (40 cm fall, 1 000 mL cylinder, slow-motion camera).  A competent peer could repeat the work, but minor ambiguities remain (exact temperature of water, camera frame rate, position of the 40 cm mark, taping method).  Hence reproduction is possible âwith few ambiguities,â matching the 3-4 band.

Velocity calculations are arithmetically correct, and a power fit is applied.  Yet the processing uses average velocity rather than true terminal velocity, and sig.-fig consistency is weak.  These are âsignificant inaccuracies,â so the strand falls in the 3-4 band, lower end.

For every weakness, the student proposes practical, detailed improvements (wider/longer container, PVC pipe, string retrieval) and clearly argues how each would mitigate the specific error.  This moves beyond a mere list and meets the âexplainedâ requirement of the 5-6 band.

Raw-data tables are neatly organised, units and uncertainties are stated, processed data are presented in a second table, and a graph with axes, units and a fit is supplied.  Minor imprecision (mixed Â±0.1 s and Â±0.05 s, velocity reported to only one sig. fig.) prevents it from being fully precise, but communication is certainly clear, satisfying band 3-4.

The conclusion references the theoretical square-root relationship derived earlier and comments on why the data deviate (lack of distance, collisions).  This is a ârelevant comparisonâ to accepted scientific context, but the justification remains brief, fitting the 3-4 band.

The student states that heavier masses show slightly higher velocities and relates this to the hypothesis, noting the weakness of the trend.  The conclusion is logically drawn from the processed data but is limited by the dataâs shortcomings; it therefore meets âdescribed but not fully consistent,â earning the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The studentâs personal anecdote effectively engages the reader and frames the research question within a relatable context, enhancing motivation.
The research question clearly states the independent and dependent variables and is appropriately focused, facilitating targeted data collection.
Uncertainties vary between Â±0.1s and Â±0.05s without clear justification, reducing confidence in uncertainty analysis.
The title precisely identifies both the mass and terminal velocity variables, setting a clear focus for the investigation.
While the terminal velocity concept is well described, add references to reputable sources or curriculum documents to strengthen scientific grounding.
The list clearly labels each parameter in the drag equation, demonstrating precise communication of variable definitions.
Show the intermediate algebraic steps when substituting $$Fd$$ and $$Fb$$ into $$Fd+Fb=Fg$$ to improve the logical flow of the derivation.
The use of the infinity symbol (\\(\\infty\\)) for proportionality is incorrect; replace it with the proportionality symbol (\\(\\propto\\)).
The independent and dependent variables are explicitly defined in one sentence, promoting clarity in experimental design.
Justify the choice of the drag equation beyond Stokesâ law by discussing flow regime (Reynolds number) to confirm its applicability.
State the assumptions (e.g., turbulent vs. laminar flow) under which the drag equation $$Fd=\tfrac12\rho v^2CdA$$ is valid to clarify its use.
Changing the measurement method mid-experiment reduces reproducibility; predefine how terminal velocity will be determined to avoid ambiguity.
Ensure each tableâs column headings fully appear and consider adding quantitative ranges for controlled variables (e.g., temperature range).
Quantify the frequency or magnitude of wall collisions and tape inconsistencies to evaluate their systematic impact on results.
Specify the camera frame rate and resolution in the procedure so that timing measurements can be replicated precisely.
The sample calculation is presented as a continuous block of text and equations, making it hard to follow; break it into sequential steps.
Velocity values are reported to one significant figure, inconsistent with measurement precision; align sig-figs with uncertainty values.
When averaging, propagate uncertainty accordingly and report the combined uncertainty rather than carrying the largest single uncertainty only.
Discuss why average velocity was used instead of estimating the true terminal speed from steady-state intervals; this impacts data validity.
Include axis labels with units, a legend for the power-fit curve and annotate the correlation coefficient directly on the graph.
Quantify the strength of the trend by comparing predicted vs. actual velocity ratios or including confidence intervals in the conclusion.
The link between the low correlation coefficient (0.79) and experimental limitations shows awareness of data-model consistency.
Separate limitations and suggested improvements into distinct sections or tables to improve clarity and reader comprehension.
The retrieval string suggestion is realistic and considers potential side effects, reflecting comprehensive evaluation.
Consider discussing resource or environmental considerations (e.g., water disposal) when recommending a new container to address ethical or practical constraints.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
